Defendant Merced Girard Residential Care Facilitys Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Fourth Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-01-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT MERCED (GIRARD) RESIDENTIAL CARE FACILITY's Motion To Strike Plaintiff's Fourth Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to strike is denied.

"An amended complaint relates back to the original complaint, and thus avoids the statute of limitations as a bar ... if it: (1) rests on the same general set of facts as the original complaint; and (2) refers to the same accident and same injuries as the original complaint. "Honig v. Financial Corp. of America (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 960, 966."

Judge Karnow's August 26, 2022, order stated, "Merced offers no support for its argument that the elder neglect and wrongful death claims were 'adjudicated' in its favor when plaintiff filed the third amended complaint" and Plaintiff was then granted leave to amend. Plaintiff's fourth amended complaint relates back to her original complaint and is therefore not time-barred.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Defendants' Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Fourth Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-01-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS HAFCO ELDER CARE, INC., and HAFCO SENIOR CARE, INC.'s Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Fourth Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to strike is denied.

"Abuse of an elder or a dependent adult" means any of the following:
(1) Physical abuse, neglect, abandonment, isolation, abduction, or other treatment with resulting physical harm or pain or mental suffering.
(2) The deprivation by a care custodian of goods or services that are necessary to avoid physical harm or mental suffering.
(3) Financial abuse, as defined in Section 15610.30. (Welf. & Inst. Code sec. 15610.07.) Under Welf. & Inst. Code sec. 15610.57, neglect is defined as the "negligent failure of any person having the care or custody of an elder or a dependent adult to exercise that degree of care that a reasonable person in a like position would exercise."

Defendants argue that "Plaintiff does not make an argument that follows the definition of care custodian under the Welfare & Institutions Code." (Mtn., 5:16-17.) Plaintiff pleaded elder neglect by Merced, the facility that was charged with her father's care, and also against Defendants on multiple theories of derivative liability. (Cmplnt., 5-30.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-01-2022 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS HAFCO ELDER CARE, INC. AND HAFCO SENIOR CARE, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 4TH AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' demurrer to fourth amended complaint is overruled.

"Abuse of an elder or a dependent adult" means any of the following:
(1) Physical abuse, neglect, abandonment, isolation, abduction, or other treatment with resulting physical harm or pain or mental suffering.
(2) The deprivation by a care custodian of goods or services that are necessary to avoid physical harm or mental suffering.
(3) Financial abuse, as defined in Section 15610.30. (Welf. & Inst. Code sec. 15610.07.)

Plaintiff alleges that when she arrived at the Merced facility to see her father, she found him deceased and strapped to a chair. (Frth. Am. Cmplnt., 15:7-8.) Plaintiff alleges that Merced is an alter ego of Hafco Corporate Defendants and thus, they are responsible for Merced's wrongdoing. (Id. at 6.) Under First Western Bank & Trust Co. v. Bookasta (1968) 267 Cal.App.2d 910, 914-915, Plaintiff must show "that the corporation is not only influenced and governed by that person, but that there is such a unity of interest and ownership that the individuality, or separateness, of the said person and corporation has ceased." (Cam-Carson, LLC v. Carson Reclamation Authority (2022) 82 Cal.App.5th 535, 550 ["Alter ego liability is not limited to the parent-subsidiary corporate relationship; rather, under the single-enterprise rule, liability can [also] be found between sister [or affiliated] companies."].) Plaintiff sufficiently argues that the following demonstrates Merced's status as an alter ego of Defendants': that Defendants directly control Merced through the officers, directors, managers and shareholders; that their board of directors are believed to be substantially identical; that Defendants perform all accounting functions for Merced and entirely control its finances; that Defendants and Merced have the same agent for process of service; and that Merced's administrator directly reports to individuals from Hafco. (Frth. Am. Cmplnt., 6-8.) On demurrer, Defendants argue that Plaintiff did not plead the second requirement of alter ego liability under First Western Bank, which requires the pleading to show the promotion of injustice by the fiction of corporate separate existence. However, on general demurrer, the test is whether the complaint states any valid claim, not whether it is the claim plaintiff intended. (Quelimane Co. v. Stewart Title Guaranty Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 38-39.) Plaintiff also alleges direct liability, joint venture, aider and abettor, and conspiracy, which Defendants do not address. (Frth. Am. Cmplnt., 5-8, 13-20.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA DEMURRER TO SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The Regents of the University of California's demurrer to the wrongful death cause of action of plaintiff's second amended complaint is overruled. Plaintiff's father died April 7, 2019. The Regents say this initiated the CCP 340.5 one-year statute of limitation, because plaintiff "discovered" her father's death that day. (See id.) Plaintiff then did not file her original complaint until April 6, 2021. However, plaintiff asserts that the one-year statute does not apply as she still has not discovered - because defendants are concealing - the causes of her father's death, i.e., the facts essential to the wrongful death claim. (See Jolly v. Eli Lilly & Co. (1988) 44 Cal.3d 1108, 1110-11.) This suffices at the pleading stage.

Order signed.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT:

Demurrer 1: Overruled. Under California's relaxed pleading standards, pro per plaintiff's April 6, 2021 complaint sufficed to plead "elder abuse" within the two-year statute of limitations. (See id. at third and fourth pages.)

Demurrer 2: Overruled. The first amended complaint adequately pleads a cause of action for elder abuse. (See id. at 12:1-18:16.)

Demurrer 3: Sustained with leave to amend. Plaintiff may plead facts demonstrating that the CCP 340.5 one-year statute of limitations does not apply to her wrongful death cause of action if she can do so in good faith.

As to punitive and trebled damages, the Regents filed no motion to strike.

Ms. Hannington stated she will prepare a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling.
